title: Booking the New 2026 Tips and Overtime Deductions in QuickBooks Online
excerpt: Set up QBO payroll for the 2026 OBBBA tips and overtime deductions: W-2 codes TP and TT, split payroll items, journal entries, and phase-outs.

@graph.headline: Booking the New 2026 Tips and Overtime Deductions in QuickBooks Online
@graph.description: How to set up QuickBooks Online payroll for the OBBBA qualified tips and overtime deductions: W-2 codes TP and TT, payroll items, journal entries, and phase-outs.

mainEntity.name: Who actually claims the tips and overtime deductions, the employer or the employee?

acceptedAnswer.text: The employee claims both deductions on the new Schedule 1-A filed with Form 1040. The employer never deducts anything extra and the wages themselves do not change. The employer's job is reporting: for tax year 2026, qualified tips go in W-2 Box 12 under code TP and qualified overtime premium goes under code TT, and tipped employees also get a Treasury occupation code in Box 14b. If those amounts are wrong or missing, the employee's deduction is hard to support.

mainEntity.name: Do the deductions reduce Social Security and Medicare taxes?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Per IRS guidance as of mid 2026, these are federal income tax deductions only. Qualified tips and overtime remain subject to Social Security and Medicare withholding, and they stay in W-2 Box 1 wages. The Box 12 codes identify the potentially deductible portion; they do not exclude it from wages. Payroll journal entries in QuickBooks Online therefore look the same as before, just with more granular wage accounts and payroll items.

mainEntity.name: What counts as qualified overtime for the W-2 Box 12 code TT amount?

acceptedAnswer.text: Only the premium portion of overtime required by the federal Fair Labor Standards Act, meaning the half in time and a half. If an employee earns $20 per hour and $30 per overtime hour, only the extra $10 per overtime hour is qualified. Overtime required only by state law, daily overtime rules, collective bargaining agreements beyond FLSA, or voluntary employer premiums does not qualify and should not be coded to TT.

mainEntity.name: What are the income limits on the 2026 tips and overtime deductions?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both deductions begin phasing out when modified adjusted gross income exceeds $150,000 for single filers or $300,000 for joint filers. The tips deduction is capped at $25,000 per return per year. The overtime deduction is capped at $12,500 for single filers and $25,000 for joint filers. Both apply for tax years 2025 through 2028 and are available whether or not the taxpayer itemizes.

content:
Booking the New 2026 Tips and Overtime Deductions in QuickBooks Online

Under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), workers can deduct up to $25,000 per year in qualified tips and up to $12,500 per year ($25,000 for joint filers) in qualified overtime premium pay from federal taxable income, for tax years 2025 through 2028. The deduction is claimed by the employee, on the new IRS Schedule 1-A attached to Form 1040. The employer deducts nothing extra. But starting with tax year 2026, the employer must separately report qualified tips and qualified overtime on Form W-2 using new Box 12 codes TP and TT, plus a Treasury occupation code in Box 14b for tipped workers. That turns a personal income tax provision into a year-round payroll accounting job, and for QuickBooks Online users it means the chart of accounts and payroll item setup you run today decides whether the January 2027 W-2s are right.

This guide covers both sides: what the employer must configure in QuickBooks Online payroll now, and what the bookkeeper must track through the year so the amounts hold up. It sits alongside the other big OBBBA bookkeeping change we covered in our guide to recording 100 percent bonus depreciation in QuickBooks Online.

What the deductions are, in plain terms

Two separate deductions, per IRS guidance as of mid 2026:

Qualified tips. Voluntary cash or charged tips received from customers, or through tip sharing, in an occupation that customarily and regularly received tips. The deduction is capped at $25,000 per year. Mandatory service charges, like an automatic 20 percent added to a large party check, are not tips at all. They are service charges, taxed as regular wages, and they never qualify.

Qualified overtime. Only the premium portion of overtime compensation required by the Fair Labor Standards Act. That is the half in time and a half. A cook earning $20 per hour is paid $30 for each overtime hour; only the extra $10 is qualified. The cap is $12,500 for single filers and $25,000 for married filing jointly.

Three constraints shape everything downstream:

  • Both deductions phase out once modified adjusted gross income passes $150,000 ($300,000 for joint filers).
  • Both are income tax deductions only. Social Security and Medicare taxes still apply to every dollar of tips and overtime, and the amounts stay inside Box 1 wages on the W-2. Nothing is excluded from pay.
  • Both run for tax years 2025 through 2028. For 2025 the IRS gave employers transition relief on reporting, and employees could estimate from pay stubs. For 2026 there is no such cushion: the finalized 2026 Form W-2 makes the new codes part of standard reporting.

So the mechanics are: the employer reports, the employee deducts. Which means the quality of the employee's deduction is only as good as the employer's payroll records. That is where QuickBooks Online setup matters.

What employers must set up in QuickBooks Online payroll

The 2026 W-2 asks for three things QuickBooks Online will only produce if the payroll items are structured correctly all year:

  • Box 12 code TP: total qualified tips reported by the employee during the year.
  • Box 12 code TT: total qualified overtime compensation, meaning the FLSA premium portion only.
  • Box 14b: the Treasury-assigned tipped occupation code for the employee's job.

Here is the setup work, in order.

1. Split overtime into two payroll items. Most QBO payroll files carry a single Overtime Pay item that pays 1.5x and posts to one wage expense account. That structure cannot produce a TT amount without a year-end spreadsheet archaeology project. Replace it with two items: an overtime base item that pays the straight-time rate for overtime hours, and an overtime premium item that pays the 0.5x portion. Map the premium item to its own expense account, something like 6015 Wages - OT Premium. QuickBooks Online Payroll has been rolling out OBBBA-aware earning types; whatever your payroll version calls them, the accounting requirement is the same: the premium must be separately stated per paycheck, per employee, per year.

2. Keep FLSA overtime separate from everything that merely looks like overtime. Only FLSA-required premium qualifies. If you operate in a state with daily overtime, pay contractual double time, or hand out voluntary weekend premiums, those amounts need their own payroll items mapped away from the TT bucket. Coding California daily overtime into TT overstates the employee's deduction and invites an amended W-2.

3. Separate tip categories. Keep distinct payroll items for cash tips reported (employee already holds the money; the item runs through payroll for tax withholding only), charged tips paid out through payroll, and service charges. The first two can feed the TP total. Service charges must not.

4. Assign occupation codes. Treasury published a list of occupations that customarily and regularly received tips, each with a code that lands in Box 14b. Record the code on each tipped employee's profile now, not in January.

5. Confirm the W-2 mapping before Q4. Run a mid-year payroll details report, trace one tipped employee and one overtime-heavy employee from paycheck to year-to-date totals, and verify the premium and tip figures you expect to see under TP and TT are accumulating in their own columns. If they only exist blended inside gross pay, fix the items now while the year can still be recoded.

What the bookkeeper must track all year

It is tempting to file this under "payroll software problem" and move on. Resist that. The deduction lives or dies on records the bookkeeper controls:

  • Tip reports. Employees must report cash tips to the employer (the long-standing $20 per month threshold still applies for FICA purposes). If tip reporting is sloppy, the TP amount is wrong, and the employee either loses deduction they earned or claims deduction the records cannot support.
  • POS to payroll reconciliation. Charged tips flow from the point of sale system into a tips payable liability, then out through payroll. Reconcile that liability monthly. A tips payable account that never clears to zero is the classic sign that tips collected and tips paid are drifting apart, and the drift lands in the W-2.
  • Overtime premium by employee. The general ledger gives you the company-wide premium total; the W-2 needs it per employee. Spot check the payroll register quarterly: hours over 40 in a workweek times half the regular rate should tie to the OT premium item.
  • The regular rate itself. FLSA overtime is computed on the regular rate, which includes nondiscretionary bonuses and commissions, not just the hourly wage. If a client pays production bonuses, the premium is slightly higher than 0.5x the base hourly rate, and the TT amount should reflect that.

None of this changes the income tax the employer owes. All of it changes whether the employee's Schedule 1-A survives a mismatch letter. For firms running books for dozens of hospitality clients, this is exactly the kind of recurring, cross-client control that belongs in a standard monthly checklist rather than a heroic January effort. We wrote about operating that way at scale in how accounting firms deliver consolidated reports across 50 QuickBooks clients.

A worked payroll journal entry

Take a restaurant server on a biweekly payroll. Regular rate $12 per hour, 80 regular hours, 6 overtime hours, and $850 of charged tips collected from customers and paid through payroll. The pieces:

  • Regular wages: 80 x $12 = $960.00
  • Overtime base: 6 x $12 = $72.00
  • Overtime premium (the qualified part): 6 x $6 = $36.00
  • Charged tips paid through payroll: $850.00
  • Gross pay: $1,918.00

Assume withholdings of $160.00 federal income tax, $118.92 Social Security (6.2 percent of $1,918), $27.81 Medicare (1.45 percent), and $50.00 state income tax. Note that Social Security and Medicare are computed on the full $1,918, tips and premium included; the new deductions change none of this. The payroll journal entry:

AccountDebitCredit
6010 Wages - Regular$960.00
6014 Wages - OT Base$72.00
6015 Wages - OT Premium$36.00
2210 Tips Payable$850.00
2100 Federal Income Tax Withheld$160.00
2110 Social Security Payable (employee)$118.92
2120 Medicare Payable (employee)$27.81
2130 State Income Tax Withheld$50.00
1000 Cash (net pay)$1,561.27

Debits total $1,918.00 and credits total $1,918.00. The tips debit hits Tips Payable, not wage expense, because the restaurant already recorded the expense side when the POS batch posted the tips as a liability owed to staff. The employer then books its own payroll tax entry: debit payroll tax expense $166.73 for the matching $118.92 Social Security and $27.81 Medicare plus, say, $20.00 of FUTA and SUTA, credit the corresponding liabilities. Restaurants should also keep claiming the FICA tip credit on Form 8846; the new employee deduction does not replace it.

What did the new law change in this entry? One thing only: line 6015. The $36.00 premium now lives in its own account and its own payroll item, so by December the year-to-date balance of that item, per employee, is the code TT amount, and the year-to-date reported tips feed code TP. No re-derivation, no estimates.

Phase-outs and edge cases

The phase-out is per return, not per job. Deductions shrink once MAGI passes $150,000 single or $300,000 joint. A server married to a software engineer can find the household near the joint threshold even though the server's own W-2 looks modest. The employer still reports the full TP and TT amounts; the phase-out math happens only on Schedule 1-A.

Multiple jobs stack. An employee with two tipped jobs gets two W-2s with TP amounts, and the caps of $25,000 for tips and $12,500 or $25,000 for overtime apply to the combined total on one return. Each employer reports its own numbers without regard to the other.

Tipped and overtime in the same paycheck. Common in hospitality, and the worked example above shows it: the two amounts accumulate independently and land in two different codes. One subtlety: for a tipped employee paid the federal tip credit wage, the FLSA overtime premium is computed off the full minimum wage rather than the $2.13 cash wage, so do not let anyone compute the premium as 0.5x the cash wage.

Married filing separately generally loses the deductions, and the return must include a Social Security number. Worth flagging to any tipped client who habitually files separately.

Self-employed and 1099 workers can also have qualified tips (reported on Form 1099 or Form 4137 pathways), with an extra limit tied to the profitability of the business, and the tips must come from a business that is not a specified service trade or business. If your client shop includes salon booth renters or delivery drivers, their records burden is on them, but the same qualified versus unqualified distinctions apply.

What to tell clients now: a checklist

Send every employer client with tipped or overtime-eligible staff a short note covering:

  1. We are splitting your overtime payroll item into base and premium effective this quarter, so 2026 W-2s can carry the code TT amount.
  2. Only FLSA overtime premium qualifies. State daily overtime and contractual premiums will be tracked separately.
  3. Service charges are not tips. If your POS mixes auto-gratuities into the tip pool, we need to separate them.
  4. Tipped employees need a Treasury occupation code on file. We are adding these to payroll profiles now.
  5. Employee cash tip reporting matters more than it used to. Under-reported tips now cost your staff a real deduction, not just FICA accuracy.
  6. Withholding tables have not fully caught up in every case; employees expecting big refunds from these deductions should check their W-4 rather than assume.
  7. Nothing changes for your payroll cost or FICA. This is employee-side income tax relief with employer-side reporting duty.

Take it with you: the free 2026 Tip and Overtime Deduction Estimator runs the caps and phase-outs per employee.

Frequently asked questions

Who actually claims the tips and overtime deductions, the employer or the employee?

The employee claims both deductions on the new Schedule 1-A filed with Form 1040. The employer never deducts anything extra and the wages themselves do not change. The employer's job is reporting: for tax year 2026, qualified tips go in W-2 Box 12 under code TP and qualified overtime premium goes under code TT, and tipped employees also get a Treasury occupation code in Box 14b. If those amounts are wrong or missing, the employee's deduction is hard to support.

Do the deductions reduce Social Security and Medicare taxes?

No. Per IRS guidance as of mid 2026, these are federal income tax deductions only. Qualified tips and overtime remain subject to Social Security and Medicare withholding, and they stay in W-2 Box 1 wages. The Box 12 codes identify the potentially deductible portion; they do not exclude it from wages. Payroll journal entries in QuickBooks Online therefore look the same as before, just with more granular wage accounts and payroll items.

What counts as qualified overtime for the W-2 Box 12 code TT amount?

Only the premium portion of overtime required by the federal Fair Labor Standards Act, meaning the half in time and a half. If an employee earns $20 per hour and $30 per overtime hour, only the extra $10 per overtime hour is qualified. Overtime required only by state law, daily overtime rules, collective bargaining agreements beyond FLSA, or voluntary employer premiums does not qualify and should not be coded to TT.

What are the income limits on the 2026 tips and overtime deductions?

Both deductions begin phasing out when modified adjusted gross income exceeds $150,000 for single filers or $300,000 for joint filers. The tips deduction is capped at $25,000 per return per year. The overtime deduction is capped at $12,500 for single filers and $25,000 for joint filers. Both apply for tax years 2025 through 2028 and are available whether or not the taxpayer itemizes.

Where FinBoard fits

Once the payroll items are split, the ledger holds everything you need: premium wages by account, tips payable roll-forwards, wage expense by class or location. The remaining work is watching it monthly across every client instead of discovering problems in January. FinBoard.ai connects directly to QuickBooks Online and turns those checks into live reports: per-account drill-downs on the OT premium and tips accounts, month over month comparisons that flag a tips payable balance that stopped clearing, and consolidated views across all the client files your firm manages. Connect a client file and see the 2026 wage detail the way the W-2 will need it, all year long.
